<!-- Topic 3: Dereferencing Pointers -->
<!-- Slides 28–42 -->

# Dereferencing Pointers
<!-- Slide 28 -->

## How Do We Follow an Address? {.smaller}

- A pointer stores where an object lives
- Dereferencing accesses the object at that location

::: notes
Slides 28–42
:::

<!-- Slide 29 -->

---

## The Dereference Operator

```{.cpp}
int score = 88;
int* ptr = &score;

cout << *ptr << "\n";
```

- `*ptr` means the object located at the address in `ptr`

<!-- Slide 30 -->

---

## Reading Through a Pointer

```{.cpp}
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    int score = 88;
    int* ptr = &score;
    cout << score << "\n";
    cout << *ptr << "\n";
    return 0;
}
```

<!-- Slide 31 -->

---

## Indirect Access Is Not a Copy

```{.cpp}
int score = 88;
int* ptr = &score;

*ptr = 95;

cout << score << "\n"; // 95
cout << *ptr << "\n";  // 95
```

<!-- Slide 32 -->

---

## Writing Through a Pointer

```{.cpp}
int balance = 100;
int* balancePtr = &balance;

*balancePtr += 25;
cout << balance << "\n"; // 125

balance -= 10;
cout << *balancePtr << "\n"; // 115
```

<!-- Slide 33 -->

---

## One Object, Multiple Access Paths

```{.cpp}
int value = 10;
int& alias = value;
int* ptr = &value;

alias = 20;
*ptr += 5;

cout << value << " " << alias
     << " " << *ptr << "\n";
```

<!-- Slide 34 -->

---

## Operators Depend on Context

```{.cpp}
int product = 6 * 7; // multiplication
int value = 42;
int* ptr = &value;   // pointer declaration
cout << *ptr;        // dereference
```

- Read the surrounding expression before interpreting `*`

<!-- Slide 35 -->

---

## Dereferenced Values Behave Normally

```{.cpp}
int value = 8;
int* ptr = &value;

*ptr += 2;
(*ptr)++;

if (*ptr > 10) {
    cout << *ptr << "\n";
}
```

<!-- Slide 36 -->

---

## Input Through a Pointer

```{.cpp}
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    int age = 0;
    int* destination = &age;
    cout << "Age: ";
    cin >> *destination;
    cout << age << "\n";
    return 0;
}
```

<!-- Slide 37 -->

---

## Selecting Data at Runtime

```{.cpp}
int regularPrice = 40;
int salePrice = 28;
bool saleIsActive = true;

int* price = &regularPrice;
if (saleIsActive) {
    price = &salePrice;
}

cout << "Price: $" << *price << "\n";
```

<!-- Slide 38 -->

---

## A Pointer Can Change the Selected Object

```{.cpp}
int first = 5;
int second = 10;
int* target = &first;

*target *= 2;
target = &second;
*target *= 2;

cout << first << " " << second << "\n";
```

<!-- Slide 39 -->

---

## Tracing Indirect Assignment

```{.cpp}
int points = 12;
int* ptr = &points;
*ptr += 5;
```

1. Read the address stored in `ptr`
2. Follow it to `points`
3. Read `12`, add `5`, and store `17`

<!-- Slide 40 -->

---

## Dereference Only a Valid Pointer

```{.cpp}
int* ptr = nullptr;

if (ptr != nullptr) {
    cout << *ptr << "\n";
} else {
    cout << "No object selected\n";
}
```

<!-- Slide 41 -->

---

## Dereferencing Unlocks Indirect Access

- Reading `*ptr` reads the selected object
- Assigning to `*ptr` changes that same object
- Reseating the pointer changes which object the code selects

<!-- Slide 42 -->
